en.extract:
The pilotbird (Pycnoptilus floccosus) is a species of passerine bird in the family Acanthizidae. It is monotypic within the genus Pycnoptilus.  The species is endemic to south-eastern Australia.


Taxonomy
The species was first described by English ornithologist John Gould in 1851. The generic name Pycnoptilus derives from the Ancient Greek pyknos 'thick' and ptilon 'feather'. Its specific epithet floccosus is Late Latin for 'flocked with wool'. There are two subspecies: the nominate subspecies Pycnoptilus floccosus floccosus lives in alpine areas; and P. f. sandfordi lives in lowland forest.


Description
The pilotbird is a large, plump species of acanthizid, measuring around 18 centimetres (7.1 in) in length and weighing 27 grams (0.95 oz). The plumage of the underparts is reddish-brown with scalloping on the chest and the centre of the belly speckled dull white. The upperparts are chocolate-brown. 
The bill is short and finely pointed. Its tail is broad and semi-erect, and flicked up and down when feeding.


Distribution and habitat
The pilotbird is found from the Wollemi National Park and Blue Mountains National Park in New South Wales through to the Dandenong Ranges, near Melbourne in Victoria. Its natural habitat is temperate wet sclerophyll forests and occasionally temperate rainforest, where there is dense undergrowth with abundant debris. It is sedentary and common.


Behaviour
Its name comes from its supposed habit of following lyrebirds, taking prey that they flush, and also from its call guiding bushmen seeking for lyrebirds. This habit is well known but seldom observed. The pilotbird is highly terrestrial, feeding on or near the ground and when disturbed it runs swiftly on strong legs. Its call has been described as "piercing and sweet". The male makes a far-carrying call of wit-wit-weet-WHEER to which the female may respond with a softer wit-a-wit-ee.


Breeding
The breeding season for the pilotbird is from August to December. The globular nest is built with a side-entrance and hidden amongst the accumulated debris on the forest floor. It is an untidy construction of bark, ferns, dead leaves and rootlets. A clutch of usually two eggs, each measuring 27 by 20 mm (1.06 by 0.79 in) and varying in colour from grey-green to purple-brown, is incubated by the female for 20–22 days. During the nestling period, which lasts 14–17 days, the male will often feed the female or help with the care of the nestlings, if the female has a subsequent brood. The nests are sometimes parasitised by the fan-tailed cuckoo (Cacomantis flabelliformis).


Conservation
The pilotbird is fairly common within its small range, which has reduced as a result of forest clearance and urban development, but much of its habitat is within national parks or reserves. Its habitat is also threatened by climate change, severe weather and bushfires; nevertheless, the most recent assessment in October 2016 classified the pilotbird as vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. The pilotbird was listed as Vulnerable under the EPBC Act in 2022, in response to the 2019/2020 Australian Bushfires, which burnt 47% of its distribution, and contributed to an estimated 30-50% decline in the population.


References

The '''pilotbird''' ('''''Pycnoptilus floccosus''''') is a species of passerine [[bird]] in the family [[Acanthizidae]]. It is [[monotypic]] within the genus '''''Pycnoptilus'''''.  The species is [[Endemism|endemic]] to south-eastern [[Australia]].

==Taxonomy==
The species was first described by English ornithologist [[John Gould]] in 1851. The generic name ''Pycnoptilus'' derives from the Ancient Greek {{lang|grc-Latn|pyknos}} 'thick' and {{lang|grc-Latn|ptilon}} 'feather'.<ref name=Helm>{{cite web|url=https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling|title=Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird-names|last=Jobling|first=James A.|year=2010|access-date = 2020-04-20}}</ref> Its specific epithet ''floccosus'' is Late Latin for 'flocked with wool'.<ref name=Helm /> There are two subspecies: the nominate subspecies ''Pycnoptilus floccosus floccosus'' lives in alpine areas; and ''P. f. sandfordi'' lives in lowland forest.<ref name="Gregory">{{Cite book |doi=10.2173/bow.pilotb1.01 |chapter=Pilotbird (Pycnoptilus floccosus) |title=Birds of the World |year=2020 |last1=Gregory |first1=Phil |s2cid=216253532 |editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana }}</ref><ref name="Slater" />

==Description==
The pilotbird is a large, plump species of acanthizid, measuring around {{convert|18|cm|in}} in length and weighing {{convert|27|g|oz}}.<ref name="Gregory" /> The [[plumage]] of the underparts is reddish-brown with scalloping on the chest and the centre of the belly speckled dull white. The upperparts are chocolate-brown.<ref name="Slater">{{Cite book |last=Slater |first=Peter |title=A field guide to Australian birds. 2: Passerines / Peter Slater |date=1979 |publisher=Rigby |isbn=978-0-85179-813-4 |edition=Reprint |location=Adelaide}}</ref><ref name="Simpson">{{Cite book |last1=Simpson |first1=Ken |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ao-ZAAAACAAJ&q=0670879185 |title=Field Guide to the Birds of Australia |last2=Day |first2=N. |last3=Trusler |first3=P. |publisher=Viking |year=1999 |isbn=978-0-670-87918-2 |edition=6th |location=Ringwood, Victoria |publication-place=Penguin Books Australia |language=en}}</ref> 
The bill is short and finely pointed.<ref name="Morcombe" /> Its tail is broad and semi-erect, and flicked up and down when feeding.<ref name="Slater" /><ref name="Pizzey" />

==Distribution and habitat==
The pilotbird is found from the [[Wollemi National Park]] and [[Blue Mountains National Park]] in New South Wales through to the [[Dandenong Ranges]], near Melbourne in Victoria.<ref name=eBird>{{cite web|url=https://ebird.org/map/pilotb1|title=eBird map: Pilotbird|access-date=2020-04-20}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]] is temperate [[wet sclerophyll]] forests and occasionally temperate [[rainforest]], where there is dense undergrowth with abundant debris.<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Pycnoptilus floccosus'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22704513A93973392 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22704513A93973392.en |access-date=13 November 2021}}</ref><ref name="Morcombe" /> It is sedentary and common.<ref name="Morcombe" />

==Behaviour==
Its name comes from its supposed habit of following [[lyrebird]]s, taking prey that they flush, and also from its call guiding bushmen seeking for lyrebirds.<ref name="Morcombe">{{Cite book |last=Morcombe |first=Michael K. |title=Field Guide to Australian Birds |date=2003 |publisher=Steve Parish Pub. |isbn=978-1-74021-417-9 |language=en}}</ref> This habit is well known but seldom observed. The pilotbird is highly terrestrial, feeding on or near the ground and when disturbed it runs swiftly on strong legs.<ref name="Morcombe" /> Its call has been described as "piercing and sweet".<ref name="Pizzey">{{Cite book |last=Pizzey |first=Graham |title=A Field Guide to the Birds of Australia |date=1989 |publisher=Collins Publishers Australia |isbn=978-0-7322-2436-3 |language=en}}</ref> The male makes a far-carrying call of ''wit-wit-weet-WHEER'' to which the female may respond with a softer ''wit-a-wit-ee''.<ref name="Morcombe" /><ref name="Pizzey" /><ref name="Gregory" />

===Breeding===
The breeding season for the pilotbird is from August to December.<ref name="Morcombe" /> The globular nest is built with a side-entrance and hidden amongst the accumulated debris on the forest floor.<ref name="Morcombe" /> It is an untidy construction of bark, ferns, dead leaves and rootlets.<ref name="Pizzey" /> A clutch of usually two eggs, each measuring {{convert|27|by|20|mm|in|2|abbr=on}} and varying in colour from grey-green to purple-brown, is incubated by the female for 20–22 days.<ref name="Morcombe" /><ref name="Pizzey" /> During the nestling period, which lasts 14–17 days, the male will often feed the female or help with the care of the nestlings, if the female has a subsequent brood.<ref name="Gregory" /> The nests are sometimes [[brood parasites|parasitised]] by the [[fan-tailed cuckoo]] (''Cacomantis flabelliformis'').<ref name=Gregory/>

==Conservation==
The pilotbird is fairly common within its small range, which has reduced as a result of forest clearance and urban development, but much of its habitat is within national parks or reserves.<ref name="Gregory" /> Its habitat is also threatened by climate change, severe weather and bushfires;<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> nevertheless, the most recent assessment in October 2016 classified the pilotbird as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status 13 November 2021" /> The pilotbird was listed as Vulnerable under the [[Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999|EPBC Act]] in 2022,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Species Profile and Threats Database - Pilotbird, Pycnoptilus floccosus |url=https://www.environment.gov.au/cgi-bin/sprat/public/publicspecies.pl?taxon_id=525}}</ref> in response to the [[2019–20 Australian bushfire season|2019/2020 Australian Bushfires]], which burnt 47% of its distribution, and contributed to an estimated 30-50% decline in the population.<ref>Conservation Advice for Pycnoptilus floccosus (Pilotbird)

In effect under the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation Act 1999 from 2 March 2022.

https://www.environment.gov.au/biodiversity/threatened/species/pubs/525-conservation-advice-02032022.pdf</ref>
